hero.name: Alessandro Orlandi
hero.subtitle: For years I decided what gets built, what it costs, and who delivers it — without writing a single line of code. Since 2019, I write the code myself. I know what both sides feel like.

problems.headline: There are situations where something is clearly off —
problems.subheadline: but nobody names it precisely. These problems rarely have a technical cause.
problems.section_resolution: This is where I come in

items.title: Decisions are made too late
items.description: What problem are we actually solving? Is this the right architecture? Does it make sense to build this ourselves? These questions often only come up once implementation is already underway.
consequences: Architecture decisions get corrected retroactively — at high cost
Features get built that do not solve the actual problem
Technical debt accumulates from wrong initial assumptions
Development takes longer than planned — without a clear reason
items.resolution: I ask these questions early — when the answers can still change things. Before a single line of code is written.
outcomes: Clarity on the actual problem before solutions are discussed
Fewer retroactive corrections during the project
More realistic effort estimates from the start

items.title: Business and engineering talk past each other
items.description: Requirements get translated into technology too early. Technical concerns reach the business too late. In between, nobody understands what is really at stake.
consequences: Engineering builds what nobody actually wanted
Business goals become invisible inside technical details
Endless alignment loops without clear outcomes
Trust between both sides erodes slowly
items.resolution: I gather requirements, validate them, and translate them into technical reality — in both directions. Without information getting lost or expectations drifting apart.
outcomes: Shared understanding between business and engineering
Technical tickets with traceable business context

items.title: Short-term solutions block long-term development
items.description: Custom builds where standard solutions would work. Architecture without a view on maintainability. Scope creep from unclear requirements. The result: technical debt that eventually slows everything down.
consequences: Every change produces unexpected side effects
Onboarding new developers takes months instead of weeks
items.resolution: I think in terms of maintainability, cost, and long-term viability. Not every problem needs a custom build. Not every requirement needs to be implemented immediately.
outcomes: Deliberate build-vs-buy decisions with clear reasoning
Architecture designed with long-term cost and maintainability in mind
Predictable roadmap without a growing debt burden
Scope control from the start rather than after the fact

contact.headline: If you are reading this, you probably have a specific situation in mind.
contact.subheadline: Write to me. No pitch, no process — just a conversation.
contact.success: Message received. I will get back to you within 24h.
contact.error: Sending failed. Please reach out directly by email.

about_me.headline: How I work
about_me.lead: I do not start with technologies.
questions: What problem are we actually trying to solve?
Why is development taking longer than expected?
What assumptions were made — and which of them still hold?
Where are the friction points between business and engineering?
about_me.observation: This sounds obvious. In practice, it rarely happens.
about_me.code_body: Not because it is always necessary. But because it keeps my judgment sharp. I can estimate technical effort realistically because I carry it myself. I can evaluate architecture decisions because I know their consequences. I would not recommend a concept I could not implement myself.

impressum.title: Legal Notice
impressum.verantwortlich_heading: Responsible for content according to § 55 Abs. 2 RStV
impressum.haftung_text: The contents of this website have been created with the utmost care. However, no guarantee can be given for the correctness, completeness, and topicality of the contents. As a service provider, I am responsible for my own content on these pages in accordance with general laws pursuant to § 7 Para. 1 TMG.

datenschutz.title: Privacy Policy
datenschutz.erhebung_text: Data processing on this website is carried out by the website operator. The contact details of the website operator can be found in the legal notice. No cookies are set and no tracking technologies are used when visiting this website.
datenschutz.kontaktformular_text: If you send me requests via the contact form, your details from the inquiry form, including the contact details you provided, will be stored by me for the purpose of processing the request and in the event of follow-up questions. This data will not be passed on without your consent.
datenschutz.hosting_text: This website is hosted by Vercel. The hoster collects the following data in so-called log files transmitted by your browser: IP address, address of the previously visited website, time of the request, time zone difference to Greenwich Mean Time, content of the request, HTTP status code, amount of data transferred, website from which the request comes and information about the browser and operating system.
datenschutz.rechte_text: You have the right to receive free information about the origin, recipient and purpose of your stored personal data at any time. You also have the right to request the correction or deletion of this data. If you have given consent to data processing, you can revoke this consent at any time for the future. You can contact us at any time for this and other questions on the subject of data protection.

meta.title: Alessandro Orlandi — Senior Software Engineer
meta.description: Software engineer with an enterprise background. I build software and help decide what should be built.

hero.headline: Most software engineers come from one direction.
hero.subtitle: I started on the business and IT side. Decided what gets built. Managed budgets. Led development teams — without writing a single line of code. Then I started engineering. And brought everything I had learned on the other side with me.

people.title: HR Recruiting
people.subtitle: How organizations think about roles, teams, and talent
people.description: I started my career in recruiting — not in IT. I learned how companies think internally about roles, responsibility, and team structure. That was not a detour. It was the foundation of an organizational understanding that nobody had to teach me later.

business.title: IT Solutions Architect & Technical Product Owner
business.subtitle: Commissioning software before building it myself
business.description: I managed budgets, made make-or-buy decisions, ran vendor selection, and introduced and led a Change Advisory Board. I coordinated development teams and aligned stakeholders at management level — without writing a single line of production code. That is why I know what the other side of the table feels like.

technology.title: Lead Frontend Engineer & Fullstack Software Engineer
technology.subtitle: Learning engineering from the inside — with an outside perspective
technology.description: Since 2019 I have been building software professionally. Direct client contact across all projects, technical consulting, and responsibility for piloting new technologies. Increasingly involved in early planning phases — before implementation began. My background shaped my engineering mindset from the start: What should be built? Why? Does this make sense?

today.title: Architect, Consultant & Senior Engineer
today.subtitle: Connecting business and engineering from first-hand experience
today.description: At Evora I was the full business/engineering interface: gathering requirements with key users, translating them into technical tickets, and leading a remote development team. As an independent developer I built my own products — including DienstBuddy, a mobile app for emergency service workers. Today I am Senior Software Engineer at Peerigon.

emt.headline: Concurrently active in emergency services
emt.description: Alongside my work in software development, I continue to volunteer in emergency medical services. This experience shapes how I communicate, set priorities, and make sound decisions under pressure.

summary.headline: I am not an engineer who worked their way toward the business side.
summary.headline2: I am someone who knows both sides from the inside.
summary.subtitle: I write code and can simultaneously judge whether the right thing is being built — because I know what both feel like in practice.
